name: Zhuge Liang
description: Zhuge Liang, also known as Kongming, was a brilliant strategist, statesman, inventor, and scholar who lived during the late Eastern Han dynasty and Three Kingdoms period of China. He served as the chancellor and regent of the state of Shu Han during the Three Kingdoms period. Often romanticized as the epitome of intelligence and wisdom, Zhuge Liang is considered one of the most accomplished strategists in Chinese history. His profound knowledge of military tactics, politics, and diplomacy allowed him to lead the weaker Shu Han against the mightier states of Cao Wei and Eastern Wu. He is credited with numerous inventions, including the repeating crossbow (Chu Ko Nu) and the mantou (steamed bun). His strategic acumen was instrumental in the Battle of Red Cliffs, where he formed an alliance with Sun Quan of Eastern Wu to defeat Cao Cao's numerically superior forces. Zhuge Liang's unwavering loyalty and dedication to restoring the Han dynasty, even after the death of Liu Bei, cemented his place as a national hero. He is revered for his integrity, his strategic genius, and his unwavering commitment to his ideals. He is a symbol of loyalty, intelligence, and strategic brilliance, and his legacy continues to inspire generations.
subtitle: The Sleeping Dragon, Crouching Dragon

keyMyths.title: Meeting Liu Bei
keyMyths.description: Liu Bei visits Zhuge Liang thrice at his thatched hut to recruit him as his strategist. Zhuge Liang initially declines but eventually accepts, recognizing Liu Bei's virtue and ambition.

keyMyths.title: Borrowing Arrows with Straw Boats
keyMyths.description: During the Battle of Red Cliffs, Zhuge Liang tricks Cao Cao into providing him with arrows by sending straw-filled boats into Cao Cao's fleet under the cover of fog. Cao Cao's archers fire upon the boats, providing Zhuge Liang with thousands of arrows.

keyMyths.title: Empty Fort Strategy
keyMyths.description: Facing Sima Yi's superior forces, Zhuge Liang uses the "Empty Fort Strategy" by leaving the city gates open and playing the guqin on the city walls. Sima Yi, suspecting an ambush, retreats, saving Zhuge Liang's forces.

sources.author: Luo Guanzhong
sources.description: A historical novel that romanticizes the events of the Three Kingdoms period, featuring Zhuge Liang as a central character.
sources.text: A historical novel that romanticizes the events of the Three Kingdoms period, featuring Zhuge Liang as a central character.

sources.author: Chen Shou

associations.name: Feather Fan
associations.significance: Represents his calm demeanor and strategic thinking even in the face of adversity.

associations.name: Dragon
associations.significance: Symbolizes his wisdom, power, and connection to imperial authority, though he never claimed the throne.

associations.name: Eight Trigrams Formation
associations.significance: Represents his mastery of complex strategies and tactical formations.

associations.name: Loyalty
associations.significance: His unwavering devotion to Liu Bei and the Shu Han dynasty.

associations.name: Benevolence
associations.significance: His concern for the welfare of the people under his rule.

associations.name: Crane
associations.significance: Symbolizes longevity, wisdom, and a connection to the spiritual realm.

associations.name: White
associations.significance: Represents purity, mourning, and his commitment to serving the fallen Han dynasty.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Known for his cunning intellect and strategic prowess in warfare and diplomacy.
A wise advisor and strategist, possessing magical knowledge and guiding a ruler.
A god known for his wisdom, knowledge of runes, and strategic planning in war, often acting as an advisor.
An ancient Indian polymath who served as advisor and prime minister to Chandragupta Maurya, known for his political strategies.

worshipPractices.name: Zhuge Liang Temple
worshipPractices.description: Temples dedicated to Zhuge Liang are found throughout China, where he is venerated as a god of wisdom and strategy. Worshippers offer prayers for success, intelligence, and good fortune.

worshipPractices.name: Ancestral Rites
worshipPractices.description: Families with ancestral connections to Zhuge Liang may perform rites to honor him as an ancestor, seeking his blessings and guidance.

cultural.modernLegacy: Zhuge Liang remains a prominent figure in Chinese culture, celebrated in literature, opera, film, and video games. His wisdom and strategic thinking continue to inspire leaders and strategists today, and he is often invoked as a symbol of intelligence and integrity.

extendedContent.title: Early Life and Aspirations
extendedContent.content: Born in Yangdu County, Shandong, Zhuge Liang (181-234 AD) lost his parents at a young age and was raised by his uncle. He lived a secluded life in the countryside, dedicating himself to scholarship and cultivating his intellect. Despite his reclusive nature, Zhuge Liang was keenly aware of the political turmoil of the late Eastern Han dynasty. He observed the corruption and instability that plagued the empire, fueling his desire to find a righteous leader who could restore peace and order. He famously compared himself to Guan Zhong and Yue Yi, renowned statesmen of ancient China, revealing his lofty ambitions. Zhuge Liang's reputation for wisdom and talent eventually reached Liu Bei, a warlord seeking to establish his own power base. After three personal visits from Liu Bei, Zhuge Liang agreed to serve as his strategist, marking the beginning of a legendary partnership that would shape the course of Chinese history.

extendedContent.title: Greatest Quests and Strategies
extendedContent.content: Zhuge Liang's service to Liu Bei was marked by a series of brilliant strategic campaigns. One of his most significant achievements was the formation of the Shu-Wu alliance, uniting Liu Bei and Sun Quan against the powerful Cao Cao. This alliance secured Liu Bei's foothold in Jing Province and laid the foundation for the establishment of the Shu Han dynasty. He is also famous for the Battle of Red Cliffs, where his strategic planning contributed to the defeat of Cao Cao's numerically superior forces. Following Liu Bei's death, Zhuge Liang served as the regent for Liu Shan, Liu Bei's son. He launched several Northern Expeditions against the state of Cao Wei, aiming to restore the Han dynasty. Although these expeditions ultimately failed to achieve their objective, they demonstrated Zhuge Liang's unwavering loyalty and military genius. His use of innovative tactics and inventions, such as the repeating crossbow (Chu Ko Nu) and the 'stone sentinel maze,' further cemented his reputation as a master strategist.

extendedContent.title: Enduring Legacy and Cultural Impact
extendedContent.content: Zhuge Liang's legacy extends far beyond his military and political achievements. He is revered as a symbol of wisdom, integrity, and unwavering loyalty. His life and deeds have been immortalized in countless works of literature, drama, and art, most notably in the historical novel *Romance of the Three Kingdoms*. This novel, while fictionalized, has greatly shaped the popular perception of Zhuge Liang, portraying him as a near-mythical figure with unparalleled intellect and foresight. His famous quotes and strategies are still studied and applied in various fields, from business management to military science. Zhuge Liang's image continues to be invoked in modern media, solidifying his status as one of the most iconic and influential figures in Chinese history. Temples dedicated to Zhuge Liang can be found throughout China, where he is worshipped as a deity of wisdom and strategy, further demonstrating his enduring cultural impact.

shortDescription: A brilliant strategist and statesman of the Three Kingdoms period. Revered for his wisdom, loyalty, and innovative military tactics. An icon of Chinese culture.
